description: Diagnostic decision tree for A2338 M2 (MacBook Pro 13" M2). Follow sequentially from Step 1. Has Touch Bar/DFR (pages 257-258), fan (page 145). Two USB-C left side (ATC0/ATC1), NO MagSafe. SoC: M2 T8112.
WARNING: A2338 M2 (820-02773) MBP 13" M2 ONLY. NOT the M1 A2338 (820-02020). SoC is M2 T8112 (NOT M1 T8103). SVR AON is ~30A, 3-phase. M2-specific comparator designator (equivalent of U9810 on A2681, NOT U8110 from M1). Has Touch Bar/DFR. Has fan. NO MagSafe — USB-C only like M1 A2338. PBUS E-Fuse present (M2-specific). HV Power Support on page 54.
data_source: Schematic PNGs (pages 23-30, 52-54, 57-59, 77-88, 122-128, 145, 147, 150-155, 220-221, 230, 236-238, 245-252, 257-258) cross-referenced with board-level repair knowledge. Power-on sequence verified against Eason reference (iphone_cpu_fix) M1/M2 standby circuit PDF.

eason_reference.source: M1 series standby circuit and power-on principle, and 20V boost circuit (Eason @ iphone_cpu_fix)
power_on_sequence: 1. PPVBUS_USBC0 5V → CD3217 MOSFET → PPHV_INT0_AONSW → fuse → PPDCIN_AONSW (USB-C only, no MagSafe on A2338 M2)
2. PPDCIN_AONSW → charger IC (page 52). Charger support circuitry on page 53. HV Power Support on page 54.
3. Charger IC → PPBUS_AON (12.6V temp). Enable signal → P3V8AON_PWR_EN → U5700 SVR AON
5. PP3V8_AON_VDDMAIN (pages 57-59) → SPMU + comparator. R-divider → MPMU_SNS_DIV → comparator. When same>opposite, PMU_VDD_HI(3.8V) → MPMU voltage detect
6. MPMU → all MPMU rails (pages 81-87) + PP3V3_S2_UPC/PP1V8_S2 to CD3217. LDOs → PP3V3_AON, PP1V8_AON. SPMU (pages 77-80) → SPMU rails
7. M2 T8112 hard start (24MHz crystal + 32.768KHz crystal + PMU_RESET_L). M2 reads ROM, I2C to CD3217
8. M2 → MPMU → P5VS2_PWR_EN → PP5V_S2 (page 122). PP3V3_S2 (page 127). External switches page 128.
9. CD3217 reads shared ROM (4 SPI lines), sends PD via CC1/CC2 → charger boosts to 20V
10. 20V → CD3217 MOSFET → PPDCIN_AONSW → charger IC → real PPBUS_AON (12.6V regulated)

forced_start_bypass.description: Bypass protective isolation to test if fault is pre-SVR or post-SVR. Uses M2 A2338-specific comparator (equivalent of U9810 on A2681). Critical diagnostic shortcut from Eason reference.
steps: 1. Inject 3.8V on PP3V8_AON_VDDMAIN externally (use 4.3V if testing with display attached)
2. Jumper comparator output to force PMU_VDD_HI high (M2 A2338-specific comparator — NOT U8110 from M1)
3. Press start button OR connect charger for 3 seconds

interpretation.forced_start_works: Fault is in the FRONT END — protection isolation and 5V→20V boost circuit (CD3217, charger IC, fuses). Check Steps 1-2 of diagnostic tree.
interpretation.forced_start_fails: Fault is in the BACK END — M2 working conditions (PMU, SoC power, clocks, ROM). Check Steps 3+ of diagnostic tree.

ppvaon_fault_l.description: Single-pin SVR fault check. Active-low fault output from U5700. If low, U5700 reports internal fault — check phase FETs, inductor shorts, overcurrent. If high, SVR AON considers itself healthy.

pmu_active_ready.description: PMU finished startup? If high, MPMU has completed its boot sequence and all enabled bucks are running. If low or absent, MPMU is stuck — check PMU_VDD_HI, 32.768kHz clock, PMU_RESET_L.

crystal_32khz.description: 32.768kHz heartbeat alive? Probe the crystal oscillator for the MPMU. If no oscillation, MPMU cannot complete startup. Check for cracked crystal, cold solder, or contamination on pads.

soc_force_dfu.description: Hardware vs software differentiation. If SOC_FORCE_DFU is asserted and board enters DFU, the SoC hardware is alive but firmware/NAND is corrupt. If DFU fails, SoC or PMU hardware fault. Check PMU support page 88.

phase_balance.description: Measure voltage drop across RS710, RS711, RS712 to verify all three SVR AON phases carry equal current. Imbalanced phases indicate a dead phase driver FET (Q5800/Q5820/Q5840) or open inductor (L5800/L5820/L5840). Each sense resistor feeds back to U5700 for current balancing.

cd3217_sys_detect.description: CD3217 needs SYS_DETECT from battery to confirm system presence before enabling PD negotiation. If SYS_DETECT missing, CD3217 stays at 5V — no 20V negotiation. Check charger page 52 and charger support page 53.

pbus_efuse.description: M2-specific PBUS E-Fuse. Not present on M1 A2338 boards. Check for blown e-fuse which would cut PPBUS distribution to downstream loads. Verify continuity across e-fuse. If open, downstream short blew it — find the short before replacing.

pp5v_s2_bootstrap.description: PP5V_S2 to PP3V8_AON VDRV bootstrap path via R5900. Feeds back from S2 domain to sustain AON SVR driver voltage after initial startup. If R5900 is open or damaged, SVR AON may lose VDRV after S2 comes up, causing boot loop or intermittent shutdown.

tree.probe: Bulk capacitors near charger IC output (page 52). Check charger support (page 53) and HV Power Support (page 54).

no.diagnosis: Charger path fault — USB-C only (no MagSafe on A2338 M2)
check_sequence: Check PPVBUS_USBC0/USBC1 at USB-C connector pins (page 147) — should have 5V or 20V from adapter
Check CD3217 port controllers: ATC0 and ATC1 (pages 150-155)
Check SPI ROM for CD3217 — if corrupted, CD3217 will not negotiate PD
Check SYS_DETECT from battery connector — charger needs battery detect signal. NOTE: A2338 has soldered battery connector (like M1 variant), can crack
Check charger IC (page 52) gate drives and MOSFET switches
Check charger support circuitry (page 53)
Check HV Power Support (page 54) — high-voltage path components
Check PBUS E-Fuse (M2-specific) — if blown, no PPBUS distribution
Check input fuses — if blown, short downstream of fuse
Thermal cam on charger area for shorted FETs

tree.probe: PP3V8_AON output caps near U5700 SVR AON controller. 3V8 AON pages 57-59. Check VDDMAIN branch.

check_sequence: Check PPVAON_PWR_EN enable — derived from charger IC via delay circuit
Check PPVAON_FAULT_L (pages 57-59) — SHORTCUT: if low, U5700 reports fault condition
Check phase balance via RS710/RS711/RS712 sense resistors — imbalance indicates dead phase
Diode mode on PP3V8_AON output caps — check for short on VDDMAIN branch
Check PP3V8_AON_VDDMAIN separately from other AON branches (pages 57-59)
Check 3-phase driver FETs: Q5800 (DRVL1 via L5800), Q5820 (DRVL2 via L5820), Q5840 (DRVL3 via L5840)
Check R5900 bootstrap resistor — PP5V_S2 to PP3V8_AON VDRV path, if open SVR may not sustain
Thermal cam on U5700 and phase FETs Q5800/Q5820/Q5840 at low voltage (5V current-limited)
SHORTCUT: Try Eason forced start bypass — inject 3.8V on PP3V8_AON_VDDMAIN + jumper comparator output + press start. If board boots, fault is upstream of SVR.

tree.probe: LDO outputs near SPMU area. PP3V3_AON and PP1V8_AON output capacitors.

check_sequence: Check PP3V8_AON input to LDOs — if missing, fault is upstream at U5700 SVR AON
If PP3V3_AON missing: check LDO IC input/output, diode mode on output cap
If PP1V8_AON missing: check LDO IC input/output, diode mode on output cap
Thermal cam on LDO area for hot component indicating downstream short

tree.probe: SPMU buck output caps (pages 77-80). Multiple buck regulators with large cap banks.
tree.expect: Multiple SPMU buck rails present. Check individual buck outputs for expected voltages.

check_sequence: Check PP3V8_AON_VDDMAIN input to SPMU (pages 77-80)
Check SPMU buck enable signals on pages 77-80
Diode mode across individual SPMU buck output cap banks
Check for cap short on individual buck outputs — isolate which buck is missing
Thermal cam on SPMU area — if hot, internal short or downstream loading
If single buck missing: find downstream short on that rail before replacing SPMU

tree.probe: MPMU buck output caps (pages 81-87). Check PMU support page 88 for PMU_ACTIVE_READY, SOC_FORCE_DFU, 32.768kHz clock.
tree.expect: Multiple MPMU buck rails present — PCPU, ECPU, GPU, SOC domain voltages. PMU_ACTIVE_READY should go high.

no.diagnosis: MPMU fault — check comparator and clock first
check_sequence: Check PP3V8_AON_VDDMAIN input to MPMU (pages 81-87)

CRITICAL (Eason ref): Check comparator — R-divider feeds MPMU_SNS_DIV to comparator same-direction input. PP3V8_AON feeds opposite input. When same > opposite, comparator outputs PMU_VDD_HI (3.8V) for MPMU voltage detect. Without PMU_VDD_HI, MPMU will NOT start.
Check 32.768kHz crystal — MPMU needs this clock to start (per Eason reference). Probe for oscillation.
Check PMU_ONOFF_L — pulled up by PP1V8_AON_MPMU
Check PMU_ACTIVE_READY output (page 88) — SHORTCUT: if not going high, MPMU is stuck
Check SOC_FORCE_DFU (page 88) — if asserting, SoC hardware may be alive but firmware bad
Check PMU_RESET_L — must be released for MPMU to start
Check individual MPMU buck enable signals (pages 81-87)
Diode mode across MPMU buck output cap banks
Thermal cam on MPMU area

tree.probe: PP5V_S2 output (page 122). PP3V3_S2 output (page 127). External switches page 128.

no.diagnosis: Standalone VR enable or IC fault — S2 domain rails
check_sequence: Check P5VS2_PWR_EN enable signal (page 122) — gated by PMU GPIO, only asserted AFTER M2 hard start
Check PP5V_S2 VR IC input (PPBUS_AON), output inductor, sense resistor on page 122
Check R5900 bootstrap — if open, PP5V_S2 may come up but SVR AON VDRV drops out causing boot loop
Check PP3V3_S2 VR and P3V3S2_PWR_EN gating (page 127)
Check external switches on page 128 — downstream loads may pull S2 rails down
Diode mode on PP5V_S2 and PP3V3_S2 output caps for downstream shorts

tree.probe: SoC power pages 23-30. PCPU, ECPU, GPU, DDR, SOC domains from MPMU/SPMU bucks feeding M2 T8112.
tree.expect: Multiple voltage domains present at SoC pins. Board should attempt boot.

no.diagnosis: SoC fault or SPI ROM fault
check_sequence: Check SPI NOR flash — if M2 cannot read firmware, no boot
Check secure element
Check SoC ground connections on SoC power pages 23-30 — open ground can cause no-boot
Check NAND/storage power (pages 220-221, 230) — M2 needs storage for boot
Check SOC_FORCE_DFU (page 88) — if DFU works, SoC is alive but firmware/NAND is bad
If all power present but no Apple logo: try DFU mode via USB-C

substeps.probe: Display power and sequencing (pages 236-238). Check backlight boost, display power rails, eDP data lines.
substeps.signals: eDP data lanes, backlight PWM, display power enable sequence
substeps.note: Check display connector and flex for damage. EDP line shorts from CPU documented on A2338 M1 variant — same risk on M2.

substeps.probe: DFR Touch Bar controller and connectors (pages 257-258). Check DFR power rails and data interface.
substeps.note: Touch Bar connector is a known liquid damage hotspot on A2338. Check connector pads for corrosion. DFR power rails and I2C/SPI interface to SoC.

substeps.probe: ATC0 and ATC1 port controllers (pages 150-155). USB-C connectors on page 147. Both ports on left side.
substeps.note: No MagSafe on A2338 M2 — USB-C is the ONLY power/data path. CD3217 port controllers handle PD negotiation, data mux, and SPI ROM access. CD3217 failure is a common IC fault.

substeps.probe: Keyboard controller, matrix, and connectors (pages 250-252). Check keyboard backlight and membrane connections.
substeps.note: Keyboard is historically the dominant fault on A2338 (64% of logic board cases on M1 variant). Check for liquid damage on keyboard flex and connector.

substeps.probe: Fan connector and driver circuit (page 145). Check MOSFET driver, level shifter, PP5V_S2_TO_FAN rail.
substeps.note: A2338 has a fan (unlike A2681 Air). Fan connector area IC failure documented on M1 variant. Check for nearby component damage.

substeps.probe: Audio codec, amplifiers, and speaker connectors (pages 245-249). San Diego PMU provides audio-related power.
substeps.signals: I2S data, amplifier enables, speaker connector signals
substeps.note: Audio IC failures documented on A2338 M1 variant. Some audio IC faults are unrepairable — if IC replacement does not fix, may need to move on.

substeps.probe: WiFi/BT module and antenna connections. Check module power rails and antenna continuity.
substeps.note: WiFi/BT module is soldered — rework requires BGA skills. Check for cold solder or pad corrosion.

substeps.probe: SSD0 (pages 220-221), storage support (page 230). Check NAND power rails, SSD controller, and data lines.
substeps.note: M2 NAND is paired to SoC. If NAND fault confirmed, board may be unrepairable without SoC transplant.

substeps.probe: Trackpad controller, LDO, and connector (pages 250-252 area). Check SPI interface and power.
substeps.note: Check trackpad flex for liquid damage. Trackpad issues often co-occur with keyboard faults from liquid spills.
